title: Refining the Theory of Basic Individual Values
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Can basic individual values be represented as a more precise motivational continuum than the original ten-value theory, and does that refinement improve prediction of external beliefs?
method: The authors tested a 19-value instrument with confirmatory factor analysis, multidimensional scaling, and predictive-validity analyses across 15 samples from 10 countries.
population_or_data: 6,059 participants in 15 cross-national samples from 10 countries completed the refined values instrument and measures used for external validation.
core_finding: The analyses supported distinguishing 19 values and largely recovered their predicted circular motivational order; the narrower values also produced more specific associations with external beliefs than the original broad value categories.
architecture_implications: Represent owner-confirmed values as a structured motivational map with compatibility and conflict relations, rather than as independent flat labels.
Treat the map as an explicit self-report layer with versioning and confidence; conversational evidence may suggest an interview question but should not silently assign a value priority.
limitations: The evidence is based mainly on cross-sectional self-report instruments and belief correlates. Cross-sample structural support does not establish that a value score predicts a particular individual's behavior, remains stable over time, or transfers unchanged into private messaging contexts.

title: Values and Behavior: Strength and Structure of Relations
venue: Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin
research_question: How strongly do different basic values relate to recurrent value-expressive behaviors, and do the relations share the motivational structure proposed by values theory?
method: Three studies related measures of value priorities to common recurrent behaviors and examined the joint structure of values and behaviors.
population_or_data: Participants across three studies reported value priorities and recurrent behaviors; the primary abstract does not report sample sizes or demographic composition.
core_finding: Value-behavior links varied substantially: stimulation and tradition were strongly related to matching behavior, several values were moderately related, and security, conformity, achievement, and benevolence were only marginally related. Normative pressure was offered as one reason behavior can obscure values.
architecture_implications: Store value declarations separately from observed behavior episodes and record plausible situational or normative constraints for each episode.
Calibrate behavioral evidence by value domain instead of assuming one universal mapping from message behavior to latent values; weak mappings should remain uncertain.
limitations: The studies are correlational and rely on reported values and recurrent behavior. The abstract does not provide sample composition, and normative explanations do not identify which constraint caused any one person's behavior.

title: Forgetting All Else: On the Antecedents and Consequences of Goal Shielding
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Does activating a committed focal goal inhibit alternative goals, what moderates that inhibition, and can shielding improve focal-goal pursuit?
method: Six studies manipulated or measured focal-goal activation and commitment, assessed accessibility of alternative goals, and examined downstream pursuit and attainment.
population_or_data: Human participants, including student samples, completed six laboratory studies of goal accessibility and pursuit; exact sample sizes are not reported in the primary abstract.
core_finding: Committed focal goals reduced accessibility of alternatives, especially alternatives serving the same higher-order purpose; shielding weakened when alternatives facilitated the focal goal and was associated with better focal-goal pursuit.
architecture_implications: Maintain an explicit active-goal state with hierarchical, competing, and facilitative edges so retrieval can prioritize a focal goal without deleting temporarily suppressed alternatives.
Interpret temporary silence about another goal as possible goal shielding, not evidence that the goal has disappeared; confirm status through later observations or owner elicitation.
limitations: The studies focus on short-horizon laboratory accessibility and pursuit, often in student samples. Suppression in a task does not establish a durable personal goal hierarchy, and reported psychological moderators must not be repurposed for clinical profiling.

search_queries: site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Forgetting all else: on the antecedents and consequences of goal shielding"

title: Everyday Temptations: An Experience Sampling Study of Desire, Conflict, and Self-Control
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: How frequently and strongly do desires arise in daily life, when do they conflict with other goals, and what predicts resistance and enactment?
method: A one-week beeper-based experience-sampling study collected reports of desire strength, goal conflict, resistance, and enactment alongside personality measures.
population_or_data: 205 adults provided 7,827 reports of desire episodes over one week and completed measures including trait self-control and behavioral activation or inhibition sensitivity.
core_finding: Desires were frequent but often unproblematic. Personality variables were relatively more associated with early components such as desire strength and conflict, while situational and interpersonal factors were relatively more influential for resistance and enactment.
architecture_implications: Represent a decision episode as distinct variables for desire intensity, conflict with active goals, resistance, and eventual behavior rather than compressing all four into one preference label.
Attach time, social setting, and situational constraints to goal evidence because later action can be more context-sensitive than the initial desire.
limitations: The one-week study relies on prompted self-report and may alter awareness or behavior. It samples reported desire episodes rather than all decisions, and the observed associations do not identify a stable policy that transfers across domains or cultures.

title: The Framing of Decisions and the Psychology of Choice
venue: Science
research_question: Can equivalent decision outcomes produce systematically different choices when their gains, losses, probabilities, or consequences are framed differently?
method: The paper reports experimental demonstrations using hypothetical and real monetary choices and problems involving possible loss of human lives.
population_or_data: Human choice responses to matched decision problems presented under different outcome frames; sample sizes and participant composition are not reported in the primary abstract.
core_finding: Equivalent outcomes produced predictable preference reversals when the formulation of the decision changed, showing that an observed choice is partly a function of its frame rather than a transparent readout of stable utility.
architecture_implications: Persist the exact option wording, gain or loss reference point, and available alternatives with every inferred preference or decision-policy update.
Before promoting one choice into a stable owner preference, test an equivalent reframe or ask whether the owner endorses the choice across frames.
limitations: The demonstrations include stylized and hypothetical tasks, and the abstract does not expose sample composition or effect-size heterogeneity. Framing sensitivity in one task does not specify how a particular owner will decide in a consequential real-world setting.

title: Context-Dependent Preferences
venue: Management Science
research_question: How can a choice model explain violations of value maximization in which the relative attractiveness of two options changes when a third option is added?
method: The authors synthesize experimental choice anomalies and propose a formal model combining contingent attribute weighting from background context with binary comparisons in the local choice set.
population_or_data: Published empirical choice patterns involving third-option, attraction, and choice-set effects; the primary abstract does not describe a new participant sample.
core_finding: The model captures cases where enlarging a choice set increases an option's share, supporting the claim that evaluated value depends on both the background and the local comparison set.
architecture_implications: Bind preference evidence to the complete option set and salient comparison attributes instead of storing a context-free scalar utility for an item.
For recommendation replay, reconstruct unavailable, rejected, and reference options so the digital brain can explain when a choice may have been context-induced.
limitations: This is primarily a formal and integrative modeling contribution rather than a new field study. The model covers specific choice-set anomalies and does not establish that all preferences are context constructed or that its parameters identify one person's enduring motives.

title: "Coherent Arbitrariness": Stable Demand Curves Without Stable Preferences
venue: The Quarterly Journal of Economics
research_question: Can arbitrary initial anchors shift valuations while later responses remain internally coherent enough to look like stable demand?
method: Six experiments manipulated arbitrary anchors for familiar products and simple hedonic experiences, then measured subsequent valuations across quality or quantity changes and market-like conditions.
population_or_data: Experimental participants valued familiar products and hedonic experiences after arbitrary anchoring manipulations; the primary abstract does not report sample sizes or demographics.
core_finding: Arbitrary anchors strongly shifted initial valuations, while subsequent valuations remained coherently ordered; experience and some market mechanisms did not reliably remove the anchor's influence.
architecture_implications: Distinguish within-session consistency from calibrated absolute preference and retain the elicitation history and any numerical or social anchors attached to a valuation.
Use anchor-robust comparisons and repeated owner elicitation before treating a price, intensity, or priority estimate as a stable personal parameter.
limitations: The experiments concern laboratory valuations of consumer goods and short hedonic experiences. Artificial anchors may not approximate autobiographical goals, and the results do not imply that every coherent preference pattern lacks a stable component.

search_queries: site:academic.oup.com/qje "Coherent Arbitrariness"
"Coherent Arbitrariness" 10.1162/00335530360535153

title: How Choice Affects and Reflects Preferences: Revisiting the Free-Choice Paradigm
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Does post-choice spreading between chosen and rejected items necessarily demonstrate that choosing changed preferences?
method: The authors prove conditions under which the standard free-choice design produces spreading with unchanged latent preferences, then experimentally test conditions where spreading cannot have been caused by choice.
population_or_data: Human participants rated, ranked, or chose objects in free-choice-style experimental tasks; the primary abstract does not report total sample sizes or demographics.
core_finding: The conventional paradigm can show greater liking for chosen items and lower liking for rejected items even when preferences do not change, because initial ratings are noisy while choice partly reveals the latent preference.
architecture_implications: Do not treat post-decision justification or changed ratings as preference updating unless the inference model accounts for measurement error and the information revealed by the choice itself.
Keep pre-choice uncertainty, the selection event, and post-choice reports as separate evidence objects so true updating and selection artifacts can be tested rather than assumed.
limitations: The result invalidates a common inference from one experimental design; it does not show that choice never changes preference. The work uses controlled object-choice tasks and does not estimate the artifact's size in natural conversation or long-term decisions.

title: SSL: A Theory of How People Learn to Select Strategies
venue: Journal of Experimental Psychology: General
research_question: Can reinforcement learning explain how people select among a repertoire of inference strategies as they receive outcome feedback?
method: Four laboratory studies gave participants repeated probabilistic inference problems with feedback and compared the proposed strategy-selection learning model with extensions and an exemplar-based alternative.
population_or_data: Participants in four computerized inference experiments made repeated choices under feedback; sample sizes and demographic composition are not reported in the primary abstract.
core_finding: Participants improved with feedback and increasingly behaved in line with the best-performing strategy available in the studied environments; the strategy-selection learning model predicted their inferences better than the compared alternatives.
architecture_implications: Represent decision behavior as a context-conditioned repertoire of policies with uncertain expected performance, not as one global decision style.
Update policy weights from outcome feedback while retaining alternatives and provenance, so adaptation in one environment does not overwrite policies useful elsewhere.
limitations: The experiments use constrained probabilistic inference tasks and assume a small researcher-specified strategy repertoire. Reinforcement of task strategies may not transfer to moral, relational, or high-stakes personal decisions, and the primary abstract omits the sample composition needed to judge external validity.

title: Decision-Making Style: The Development and Assessment of a New Measure
venue: Educational and Psychological Measurement
research_question: Can recurring self-reported approaches to decisions be measured as reliable, distinguishable decision-making styles?
method: A multistage four-sample instrument-development study used principal-axis factor analysis, item analysis, internal-consistency testing, independence tests, and concurrent-validity analyses.
population_or_data: Four development and validation samples completed candidate decision-style items; the primary abstract does not report sample sizes or demographics.
core_finding: The study recovered rational, avoidant, intuitive, dependent, and spontaneous scales with a stable factor structure and reported internal consistencies ranging from .68 to .94.
architecture_implications: Use an owner-completed decision-style instrument, if desired, only as a prior over how the owner approaches a decision, while keeping observed policies and outcomes as separate evidence.
Scope style estimates by domain and context and allow multiple styles to coexist; do not turn one questionnaire score into an immutable global policy.
limitations: This is self-report scale development, not evidence that the five styles causally determine real decisions or remain invariant across domains. Sample details are absent from the abstract, and some reported internal consistencies are modest.

search_queries: site:journals.sagepub.com/doi "Decision-Making Style: The Development and Assessment of a New Measure"

title: Patterns of Cognitive Appraisal in Emotion
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Can emotions be differentiated by cognitive appraisal dimensions beyond pleasantness and arousal?
method: Participants recalled experiences associated with 15 emotions and rated each experience on proposed appraisal dimensions; dimensional analyses recovered the structure relating appraisals to emotion categories.
population_or_data: Human participants provided retrospective reports for experiences associated with 15 emotions; the primary abstract does not report sample size or demographics.
core_finding: Six dimensions emerged: pleasantness, anticipated effort, certainty, attentional activity, self-versus-other responsibility or control, and situational control; emotions varied systematically across these appraisal patterns.
architecture_implications: Represent affective episodes with appraisal variables such as certainty, control, responsibility, effort, and attention in addition to a valence or emotion label.
Do not map all negative affect to the same decision modifier: two same-valence states may imply opposite beliefs about control, responsibility, or likely action.
limitations: The study uses retrospective recall and associations between reported appraisals and reported emotions, so it cannot establish causal direction. Sample details are unavailable in the primary abstract, and the recovered dimensions may depend on the emotion list and cultural vocabulary.

search_queries: site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Patterns of cognitive appraisal in emotion"

title: Fear, Anger, and Risk
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Do same-valence emotions with different certainty and control appraisals produce different risk perceptions and choices?
method: Across studies of naturally occurring and experimentally induced fear and anger, the authors measured risk estimates, risky choices, and appraisals of certainty and control.
population_or_data: Human participants completed emotion, appraisal, and risk-judgment tasks; the primary abstract does not report the number or composition of samples.
core_finding: Fear was associated with pessimistic risk estimates and risk-averse choices, while anger was associated with optimistic estimates and risk seeking; certainty and control appraisals statistically accounted for part of the difference.
architecture_implications: Treat current appraisal and affect as transient decision context that can modulate a risk policy, rather than folding one emotional episode into a stable risk-tolerance trait.
For consequential recommendations, ask about current certainty and control and show sensitivity to alternative affective states instead of assuming a context-free policy.
limitations: The evidence uses bounded judgment tasks and induced or measured emotional states; it does not establish a durable personal policy. Sample details and cross-cultural scope are not available in the abstract, and later applications may vary by risk domain and emotion induction.

title: Feelings Change: Accounting for Individual Differences in the Temporal Dynamics of Affect
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Can individual affect trajectories be described by a personal home base, variability in perturbations, and strength of return toward that base?
method: The DynAffect account was evaluated with diffusion modeling in two intensive experience-sampling studies spanning longer and shorter time scales.
population_or_data: Participants in two experience-sampling studies repeatedly reported core affect; the primary abstract does not report sample sizes or sampling schedules.
core_finding: A model with an affective home base, variability, and attractor strength captured observed core-affect dynamics at both studied time scales and exposed meaningful individual differences related to personality and emotion dispositions.
architecture_implications: Model affect as a time-indexed latent process with a person-specific baseline, volatility, and reversion rate rather than as the latest detected label.
Keep observation intervals and uncertainty in the state update, and require repeated owner-grounded observations before estimating a personal affect-dynamics parameter.
limitations: The studies model broad core affect rather than discrete emotions and the abstract omits sample details. A good-fitting diffusion process is descriptive rather than proof of an underlying mechanism, and associations with dispositions must not be converted into clinical inference.

search_queries: site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Feelings change: accounting for individual differences in the temporal dynamics of affect"

title: Emotions in Everyday Life
venue: PLOS ONE
research_question: How often do specific emotions occur in everyday life, how often do positive and negative emotions co-occur, and how are emotion categories interconnected?
method: A francophone smartphone application prompted in-the-moment reports for 18 nonexclusive emotion labels; multilevel frequency estimates and correlation-network analyses characterized occurrence and co-occurrence.
population_or_data: 11,572 self-selected participants, 75% women and 93% French, submitted 65,721 emotion reports between February 2013 and April 2014.
core_finding: Participants reported at least one listed emotion in 90% of sampled moments and both positive and negative emotions in 33%; emotion categories differed in aggregate co-occurrence centrality, showing that mixed affect is common and that the categories have different undirected association patterns.
architecture_implications: Allow simultaneous, potentially opposite-valence affect labels and preserve an uncertainty distribution instead of forcing each moment into one mutually exclusive state.
Learn person-specific co-occurrence and time patterns only from repeated self-report; do not interpret cross-sectional emotion correlations as directed causal transitions.
limitations: The sample was self-selected, predominantly French and female, prompts could be skipped, and workday responses were overrepresented. The 18 labels were binary and researcher-selected, response propensities may depend on emotion, and the network is correlational with no direction of influence.

title: Do Facial Expressions Signal Specific Emotions? Judging Emotion From the Face in Context
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Does a prototypical face determine perceived emotion when situational information supports a different emotional interpretation?
method: Three studies paired reported facial expressions with situational descriptions and asked observers to judge the person's emotion across 22 face-context cases.
population_or_data: Adult observers judged emotions from face and situation combinations; the primary abstract does not report sample sizes or participant demographics.
core_finding: Situational information determined the judged emotion in all 22 examined face-context cases, including cases where context implied a nonbasic state such as pain despite a prototypical basic-emotion face.
architecture_implications: Require event, relationship, and conversational context before assigning affect from an emoji, phrase, or expressive cue, and expose uncertainty when context is incomplete.
Store affect classification as an observer-side interpretation distinct from the owner's internal state and prioritize direct owner correction over cue confidence.
limitations: The studies test observer judgments with constructed face-context conflicts, not the target person's ground-truth experience. The abstract omits sample details, and the results do not quantify performance for text-only, naturalistic, or cross-cultural communication.

title: GoEmotions: A Dataset of Fine-Grained Emotions
venue: Proceedings of the 58th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
research_question: Can a large, fine-grained, manually annotated text corpus support reliable multi-label emotion classification and transfer across emotion taxonomies and domains?
method: The authors curated 58,009 English Reddit comments, collected three or five crowd annotations per item over 27 emotions plus neutral, analyzed label structure and reliability, and evaluated BERT classification and transfer learning.
population_or_data: 58,009 comments sampled from 482 popular English-language subreddits were labeled by 82 native-English raters from India, with three raters per item and two more when initial raters shared no label.
core_finding: The annotation analysis supported separable fine-grained categories, but the BERT baseline reached only .46 mean F1 over 27 emotions, substantially below its performance on coarser groupings; transfer helped most when target-domain labels were scarce.
architecture_implications: Use multi-label calibrated probabilities and abstention for text affect rather than a single forced label, and validate on owner- and relationship-specific data before deployment.
Treat model output as perceived emotion expressed in text, not privileged access to the writer's internal state, because raters saw isolated comments without author or subreddit context.
limitations: Reddit and its user base are not globally representative; all raters were native-English speakers from India, filtering and taxonomy choices introduce bias, and comments were stripped of conversational metadata. Reader labels do not verify author experience, and the fine-grained baseline's .46 F1 leaves substantial error.

title: Limits on Theory of Mind Use in Adults
venue: Cognition
research_question: Do adults routinely use their explicit knowledge of another person's ignorance or false belief while interpreting that person's instructions?
method: Two referential communication experiments used a director-and-grid task in which participants knew the identity of a hidden object while the director was ignorant of it or held a false belief about it.
population_or_data: Adult participants interpreted and acted on a director's instructions in two laboratory communication experiments; the primary abstract does not report sample sizes.
core_finding: Participants often interpreted an instruction using privileged knowledge even though they explicitly knew the director lacked that knowledge, revealing a gap between reflective perspective knowledge and its routine online use.
architecture_implications: Separate an explicit model of another person's beliefs from the policy that interprets their current utterance, and test whether the policy actually respects information boundaries.
When owner and interlocutor knowledge differ, slow down reference resolution, retain competing interpretations, and ask rather than assuming that an available belief was used correctly.
limitations: The evidence comes from a tightly controlled ambiguous-reference task and does not measure all forms of adult mentalizing. The abstract omits sample details, and laboratory errors do not establish a stable inability for any particular person.

title: Perspective Taking as Egocentric Anchoring and Adjustment
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
research_question: Does perspective taking proceed by anchoring on one's own perspective and serially adjusting, and what conditions change egocentric bias?
method: Five studies combined estimates of others' perceptions, response-time comparisons, time pressure, accuracy incentives, and manipulations of when adjustment stopped.
population_or_data: Human participants completed five laboratory perspective-estimation studies; sample sizes and demographic composition are not reported in the primary abstract.
core_finding: Judgments of others began from the participant's own perspective and were often insufficiently adjusted; time pressure increased egocentric bias, accuracy incentives reduced it, and adjustment tended to stop once an estimate seemed plausible.
architecture_implications: Represent self-projection as an explicit prior in motive and belief inference, then widen uncertainty when the inferred state resembles the owner's state more than the evidence warrants.
Allocate deliberation or request clarification for consequential social predictions, because fast generation is especially likely to stop at a merely plausible self-anchored account.
limitations: The studies use bounded laboratory estimates and infer a process from behavioral signatures. Incentives reduced but did not guarantee elimination of bias, and the findings do not provide a direct accuracy benchmark for longitudinal private messaging.

title: Rational Quantitative Attribution of Beliefs, Desires and Percepts in Human Mentalizing
venue: Nature Human Behaviour
research_question: Can human judgments of another agent's beliefs, desires, and percepts be explained by probabilistically inverting a rational planning and state-estimation model?
method: A Bayesian theory-of-mind model inverted sequential planning in partially observable spatial environments and was compared with human ratings, lesioned models, and nonmentalistic motion-feature models in two experiments.
population_or_data: Experiment 1 compared model and mental-state judgments from 16 participants across spatial scenarios; Experiment 2 compared percept inferences from 176 participants across its scenario set.
core_finding: The full model closely tracked quantitative human judgments across two experiments and outperformed versions missing mental-state components and models based only on visible motion features.
architecture_implications: Use a generative latent-state model that explicitly separates what an agent can perceive, believe, and desire, then maintain a posterior over alternatives rather than writing the highest-scoring motive as fact.
Record observability and task assumptions with every inferred motive, because inverse planning is only meaningful relative to what options, costs, and information the modeled person plausibly had.
limitations: The experiments use stylized spatial agents and assume approximately rational action under a known task model; Experiment 1 is small. Agreement with human judgments does not verify the agent's actual hidden state, and everyday behavior may involve habit, coercion, conflicting goals, or unknown options.

title: Rational Inference of Beliefs and Desires From Emotional Expressions
venue: Cognitive Science
research_question: How do observers combine an agent's expected outcome, desired outcome, causal action, and emotional reaction to infer otherwise unobservable beliefs and desires?
method: Four experiments and calibration studies manipulated whether an agent wanted, expected, and caused an outcome, varied the emotional evidence shown, and compared human mental-state judgments with Bayesian model predictions.
population_or_data: Adult participants judged staged scenarios and facial reactions across four main experiments, with separate participant samples calibrating reaction likelihoods; stimuli included photographs and short acted movies.
core_finding: Observers generally recovered desires, but belief recovery depended on having sufficient and mutually consistent action and reaction evidence; when an action was improbable under the target mental states, observers often failed to recover the belief. The Bayesian model tracked judgments at correlations of at least .95.
architecture_implications: Fuse actions, expected outcomes, observed outcomes, and affective reactions in one probabilistic model while preserving multiple belief-desire explanations and their likelihoods.
When action and affect cues conflict or only one reaction is observed, surface motive uncertainty and trigger owner clarification instead of forcing a coherent motive after the fact.
limitations: The experiments restrict beliefs and desires to a very small hypothesis space and use staged expressions under shared task knowledge. Real people mask and mix emotions, cultural mappings vary, and strong fit to observer judgments does not validate the inferred hidden state as ground truth.

search_queries: site:onlinelibrary.wiley.com "Rational Inference of Beliefs and Desires From Emotional Expressions"
